title: Secrets Management
intro: A developer on your team accidentally pushed a .env file to GitHub. Imagine posting a photo of your house key online with your home address -- that's essentially what happened. The file contains your production database password and Stripe API key. This is a real emergency. What do you do?

teach.title: Secrets = House Keys
teach.body: Imagine posting a photo of your house key on social media with your home address. Anyone could copy it and walk right in. **Secrets** in code -- API keys, database passwords, encryption keys -- work the same way. If they end up in a public place like GitHub, anyone can use them to access your systems.

teach.title: Secrets = PIN Numbers
teach.body: You wouldn't write your bank PIN on a sticky note on your monitor, right? But developers accidentally do the equivalent all the time -- putting API keys and passwords directly in code that gets uploaded to GitHub. Treat every secret like your bank PIN: memorize it (store it securely), never write it where others can see it, and change it immediately if someone glimpses it.

teach.title: What Counts as a Secret?
teach.body:
These should NEVER be in your code or Git history:

- **API keys** (Stripe, AWS, SendGrid, etc.)
- **Database passwords and connection strings**
- **JWT secrets and encryption keys**
- **OAuth client secrets**
- **SSH private keys**

If someone could use it to access your systems or impersonate your app, it's a secret.

teach.title: Where Secrets Should Live
teach.body:
Instead of your code, store secrets in:

1. **Hosting platform settings** -- Vercel, Railway, and Netlify all have an "Environment Variables" section in their dashboards
2. **Local .env files** -- For development, but NEVER committed to Git
3. **Secret managers** -- For larger apps: AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Doppler
A leaked API key can drain your bank account or get your Stripe account banned overnight. Protecting secrets is how you keep making money from your vibecoded app instead of losing it.
Never put secrets in code or Git. Use .env files locally and your hosting platform's environment variables settings for production. If a secret gets leaked, rotate it immediately.

start.narrative: You get a Slack message: 'I think I accidentally pushed our .env file to GitHub.' You check and confirm -- the production database password and Stripe API key are visible in a public repository. What's your first move?

choices.text: Delete the .env file from the repo and push

rotate-first.narrative: Smart -- you immediately go to Stripe and generate a new API key, and change the database password. The old keys are now useless even if someone copied them. The leaked file is still in Git history though. What's next?

choices.text: Use BFG Repo-Cleaner (or 'git filter-repo') to scrub the .env file from the entire history, then force-push
Just delete the file in a new commit -- old commits aren't a big deal if no one looks at them

delete-only.narrative: Deleting the file with a new commit removes it from the current version, but the secret is still visible in the Git history! Anyone can browse older commits and find it. Plus, bots constantly scan GitHub for leaked secrets -- they may have already copied your keys.

choices.text: Rotate all the exposed credentials immediately

make-private.narrative: Making the repo private helps, but the secrets may have already been scraped by bots that automatically scan public repos for leaked credentials. This happens within minutes of a push. The credentials themselves need to be changed.

choices.text: Rotate all the exposed credentials immediately

choose-store.narrative: The history is clean and the old credentials are dead. Now you need a real home for the new secrets so this never happens again. Where do they live?

choices.text: In your hosting platform's Environment Variables dashboard (Vercel/Railway/Netlify)
In a dedicated secret manager like Doppler, Infisical, or AWS Secrets Manager that syncs to all environments
In a private 'config' repo so the team can clone and read them

load-locally.narrative: Production secrets are configured. Now your teammates need to run the app locally without ever putting real secrets in Git again. What's the safest local workflow?

choices.text: Commit a '.env.example' with empty placeholders, add '.env' to '.gitignore', and have each developer fill their own .env with dev-tier credentials
Share the .env file in 1Password as a single 'team .env' that everyone copies into their repo
DM the .env contents in Slack so the team can paste them in

inject-ci.narrative: Local dev is sound. Your CI pipeline runs integration tests that need a Stripe test key and a database URL. How do those secrets reach the CI runner without ever touching the repo?

choices.text: Store them in CI provider's encrypted secrets (GitHub Actions secrets, etc.) and read them as environment variables in the workflow
Echo the secrets into a temporary .env file inside the workflow, generated from inline literals committed to the YAML
Skip integration tests in CI -- just run unit tests so secrets aren't needed

audit-rotate.narrative: Secrets flow safely from store to local to CI to production. Last decision: how do you make sure a leak like this gets noticed within minutes next time, and how do you keep secrets fresh?

choices.text: Enable GitHub push protection / a pre-commit secret scanner (gitleaks, trufflehog) AND set a 90-day rotation reminder for high-value keys
Trust GitHub's automatic secret scanning to email you if anything leaks
Tell the team in standup to be careful with .env files

end-good.narrative: You contained the leak with rotation, scrubbed Git history, moved secrets into a real store, defined a clean local + CI workflow, and added active scanning plus a rotation schedule. The next leak won't even reach the remote -- the pre-commit scanner will block it.
end-good.feedback: Textbook secret hygiene. The order matters: rotate first (the leaked key is dead in seconds), clean history second (so it can't be re-leaked), then build a workflow where new secrets only ever live in the right place. Pre-commit scanning means the entire bug class is now blocked before push.

end-okay.narrative: You handled the immediate leak well and moved secrets into a proper store. Relying on GitHub's after-the-fact scanner means leaks get caught -- but only after they've already been pushed and bots have had a chance to scrape them.
end-okay.feedback: Good response. The gap is the prevention layer: GitHub's scanner catches leaks after they reach the remote, by which point bots may have already grabbed them. A pre-commit hook (gitleaks) or push protection is what actually prevents the leak rather than detecting it.

end-weak.narrative: Crisis is over, secrets are in the right place, but there's no automated check stopping the next leak. The next time someone runs 'git add .' in a hurry, the same incident will play out again.
end-weak.feedback: You did the hard recovery work but skipped the cheap prevention step. A 5-minute setup of a secret-scanning pre-commit hook would block this entire class of incident. Verbal reminders don't survive a deadline; tooling does.
